subject: [PATCH v3 0/4] mm: improve large folio readahead and alignment for exec memory

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary: Author inquired about the status of review, indicating a lack of feedback or response from reviewers.
reviews.raw_body:
Hello!

Just wanted to check if there was any feedback/review on the latest
revision? 

Thanks!

reviews.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
reviews.summary: Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes noted that the patch series should be reviewed by more people and suggested that review should be distributed more evenly across the kernel community.
reviews.raw_body:
It's -rc7, this is definitely something for next cycle :)

On my part, my upstream bandwidth has drastically reduced, and review is
probably going to have to be a hobbyist thing at least for now.

Also, not to be mean but:

$ git log -E -i --grep "(Reviewed|Acked)-by: Usama Arif" --oneline | wc -l
21

So... :)

Review in mm is very lop-sided, let's try to balance it out a bit!

reviews.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
reviews.summary: Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes noted that the patch is not being taken during the current quiet period, and instead suggested that it will be considered from around rc5 or rc6 of the next cycle.
reviews.raw_body:
(Note that we're in a 'quiet period' from here until -rc1 of next cycle and
won't be taking anything new until then. We plan to do this from around rc5 or
rc6 of each cycle in future).

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary: Author responded to Lorenzo Stoakes' feedback by explaining that they don't acknowledge patches already acked-by/reviewed-by maintainers, and instead of adding their own tags, they provided a list of recent reviews as evidence.
reviews.raw_body:
Ah yeah actually I have been reviewing a lot over the last few months.
I dont ack patches that have already been acked-by/reviewed-by maintainers.
as I am not sure if it adds anything. I never really cared about Reviewed/Acked
by/patch count but I can start adding tags if it helps in stats.

The reviews I have done over the last week alone:
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260408122307.1360475-1-usama.arif@linux.dev/ 
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260408123700.1596800-1-usama.arif@linux.dev/ 
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260409142256.131676-1-usama.arif@linux.dev/ 
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260410114809.3592720-1-usama.arif@linux.dev/ 
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260410112433.3248586-1-usama.arif@linux.dev/ 
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260331103451.1070175-1-usama.arif@linux.dev/T/#t 
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260401152343.3294686-1-usama.arif@linux.dev/

reviews.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
reviews.summary:
The reviewer expressed a neutral sentiment, stating that the patch will be revisited later due to current distractions and the introduction of LSF (Large System Filesystem), but did not provide any specific technical feedback or concerns.

Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes emphasized the importance of critically evaluating patches, assuming that maintainers can make mistakes, and actively seeking issues to improve the patch.

Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes noted that there is an imbalance between review and contribution efforts in the project, but acknowledged making a mistake in this specific case.
reviews.raw_body:
We'll come back to it! With LSF coming too I think people are fairly distracted
as well.

---

You should do that, it is meaningful. If you think a patch is OK, it's how you
say so!

Maintainers get things wrong too by the way, assume we are wrong and try to find
issues, this is how a healthy technical community operates (as long as everybody
is civil about it :)

---

It's the only metric I can quickly bring up, so sorry that in this case I got it
wrong, but the overall point remains the same - we want to see a balance of
review and contributions, right now the two are really very lopsided!

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary: Author is clarifying the timing of sending patches for review, specifically asking if it's acceptable to send patches for the next release before rc1.
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks! Just wanted to check, as I am always confused about this. Is it ok
to send patches for review for next release at this time? So that they
are in a good state when rc1 comes. I wanted to send PMD swap entries
for review after I am finished testing, but I want them for review for
next release.

reviews.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
reviews.summary: Reviewer suggested that the patch be sent during the kernel development cycle window rather than before it starts, citing potential for new material to get lost or overlooked
reviews.raw_body:
I think different people have different views on that :)

I mean it's debateable whether having a glut of new material on day one of -rc1
is preferable to having a bunch come in that might or might not get lost along
the way :)

I personally feel it'd be better to send during the cycle window rather than
before but I suspect others disagree with that!

So from your point of view, feel free to do what you like, but maybe David +
others would want to chime in with their opinions?

Thanks, Lorenzo

reviews.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
reviews.summary: Reviewer Vlastimil expressed concerns that the patch series should not be merged immediately during the quiet period, as it would create less urgency for reviewers to respond quickly and allow new series to mature before rc1.
sentiment_signals: concerns about merging patches during quiet period
reviews.raw_body:
For me the more important part of the quiet period is that patches can't be
merged, so there's less urgency to review them immediately. So I think it's
fine to still send patches, but not having expectations about quick
response, as people might be taking time off.

On the other hand it would be better if new series could mature in this
quiet period, so there would be less work after rc1. But the key to making
that possible I think is to feel less urgency/being overwhelmed also in the
non-quiet period (rc1-rc5/6). Then it's should be less necessary to take
time off during the quiet period. So hopefully we'll get there through
involving more reviewers, and by having more submaintainers agency.

Vlastimil

reviews.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
reviews.summary: Reviewer Lorenzo Stoakes expressed concerns about the risk of a patch series being taken at -rc1 without proper signoff, potentially leading to confusion and mixed-up patches. He suggested tagging series appropriately to indicate their intended destination.
reviews.raw_body:
Yeah I sympathise with that.

But until we for-sure have <you know what :))> signoff, I worry about the risk
of series 'just being taken' at -rc1 because it maybe seems easier to do that,
and then we have a series from 5 weeks ago you forgot about suddenly crop up.

So I guess the more nuanced take I have is:

Once we have a robust set up end-to-end _that can handle_ having series that are
deferred to next cycle without risk of things getting mixed up - then that makes
sense, yes.

But while there's still a bit of uncertainty around that, then I'd rather not.

But I think if people DO just resend their stuff in -rc1 then we're OK and it
addresses my concerns.

One thing we could do here is to tag series appropriately like:

[PATCH v7.2] 00/42

To make it clear where it's intended to head to.

P.S. Having the 'quiet period' REALLY REALLY helps. So thanks for that Andrew!

reviews.author: David (Arm)
reviews.summary: The reviewer, David, expressed a neutral sentiment regarding the patch, stating that he doesn't care much about reviewing new material during the quiet period and will let it sit in his inbox.
reviews.raw_body:
I personally don't care that much. People just have to be prepared that
there will be little review on new (non-fix) material during the quiet
period.

It will sit in my inbox one way or the other :)

-- 
Cheers,

David

2026-04-10.patch_summary: This patch series improves large folio readahead and alignment for executable memory in the Linux kernel. The current implementation of exec_folio_order() requests readahead at an architecture-preferred folio order, but several issues prevent it from working optimally. These include the mmap_miss heuristic disabling readahead after 100 page faults, which is not triggered on arm64 with 64K pages. This patch series addresses these issues by aligning elf segments only when necessary, removing the preferred_exec_order() function, and changing the alignment order to HPAGE_PMD_ORDER if the number of pages in a vma is greater than or equal to HPAGE_PMD_NR. Additionally, it improves checking of p_vaddr and p_vaddr, removes unnecessary checks, and updates the patch series based on feedback from reviewers.

reviews.author: Jan Kara
reviews.summary: Reviewer Jan Kara expressed concerns that relying on PMD_SIZE for VMA alignment may lead to inefficient memory usage, particularly with small machines and large folios, suggesting capping the folio size at 2MB until a more sophisticated heuristic is developed.
reviews.raw_body:
I'm not sure relying on PMD_SIZE will be too much for a VMA is a great
strategy. With 16k PAGE_SIZE the PMD would be 32MB large which would fit in
the .text size but already looks a bit too much? Mapping with PMD sized
folios brings some benefits but at the same time it costs because now parts
of VMA that would be never paged in are pulled into memory and also LRU
tracking now happens with this very large granularity making it fairly
inefficient (big folios have much higher chances of getting accessed
similarly often making LRU order mostly random). We are already getting
reports of people with small machines (phones etc.) where the memory
overhead of large folios (in the page cache) is simply too much. So I'd
have a bigger peace of mind if we capped folio size at 2MB for now until we
come with a more sophisticated heuristic of picking sensible folio order
given the machine size. Now I'm not really an MM person so my feeling here
may be just wrong but I wanted to voice this concern from what I can see...

								Honza

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged a mistake and agreed to reinstate the original logic from a previous revision, indicating that the patch will need further revision.
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks for the feedback! I agree, it makes sense. I did that in the previous
revision [1]. I will reinistante that in the next one.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260320140315.979307-3-usama.arif@linux.dev/

2026-04-13.patch_summary: This patch series improves large folio readahead and alignment for executable memory in the Linux kernel. The current implementation of exec_folio_order() requests readahead at an architecture-preferred folio order, but several issues prevent it from working optimally. These include the mmap_miss heuristic disabling readahead after 100 page faults, which is not triggered on arm64 with 64K pages. This patch series addresses these issues by aligning elf segments only when necessary, removing the preferred_exec_order() function, and changing the alignment order to HPAGE_PMD_ORDER if the number of pages in a vma is greater than or equal to HPAGE_PMD_NR. Additionally, it improves checking of p_vaddr and p_vaddr, removes unnecessary checks, and updates the patch series based on feedback from reviewers.
